title: Anthropic doubles Public First Action donation to $40M for AI policy
subtitle: Second $20M tranche to the bipartisan AI-safety education group lands two weeks before Congress returns, matching Anthropic's record Q2 federal lobbying push.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Anthropic announced on July 22, 2026 that it will contribute an additional $20 million to Public First Action, doubling its total support for the AI-safety education group to $40 million. Anthropic said the funds are restricted to public education and policy work and cannot be used to elect or defeat any candidate for federal, state, or local office. Public First Action describes itself as nonpartisan and plans to educate voters and legislators about proposed AI safeguards ahead of the 2026 midterms, with an initial focus on 30 to 50 races in both parties.

The signal here is that Anthropic is spending on both federal lobbying and outside education at the same time. The $40 million to Public First Action lands the same week the company disclosed a record $1.97 million in Q2 federal lobbying and comes as the Trump White House finalizes a 30-day voluntary frontier-review framework. That combination — insider lobbying, outside education, and cooperation with the executive branch — is the same three-track pattern the semiconductor industry used to secure CHIPS Act funding in 2022. It works because each track influences a different decision-maker.

This also widens the visible gap between Anthropic and OpenAI on how each company engages Washington. OpenAI spent $1.2 million on Q2 lobbying and has not disclosed comparable outside education spending. Anthropic has spent roughly twice as much on lobbying and forty times as much on outside advocacy. That difference is deliberate — Anthropic has framed itself as the safety-first frontier lab since founding, and the $40 million commits real money to that positioning. Public First Action's leaders include former Republican and Democratic operatives, which lets Anthropic argue safeguards are bipartisan even as the rules themselves are being written.

For learners: dark-money advocacy and c4 nonprofit spending are the parts of policy influence you never see in a press release, and they usually matter more than the lobbying-disclosure numbers that make the news. If you want to understand how AI policy actually gets shaped, follow the money in three places: registered federal lobbying (via opensecrets.org), c4 spending like this Anthropic donation, and industry-funded think-tank research. Then compare the language in each — you will often find the same phrases showing up months later in draft legislation.
